learning_objectives: Describe the four major Earth systems: atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, biosphere
Explain energy flow through ecosystems via food webs and trophic levels
Define sustainability and the concept of ecological footprint
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 1-2 by Withgott & Laposata
Ecological Footprint Calculator Background (Global Footprint Network)

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental science integrates biology, chemistry, earth science, and social science to understand how human activity interacts with natural systems. Sustainability asks whether our current practices can continue without degrading the systems we depend on.
key_concepts: Earth systems: atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, biosphere and their interactions
Ecosystems: communities of organisms interacting with their physical environment
Energy flow: producers, consumers, decomposers, and the 10% rule
Nutrient cycles: carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water
Ecological footprint: measuring human demand on Earth's resources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students calculate their personal ecological footprint using the Global Footprint Network calculator and compare results

review.value: Only about 10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next. This is why food chains rarely exceed 4-5 levels and why plant-based diets have smaller ecological footprints.
Measures the area of biologically productive land and water required to produce resources consumed and absorb waste generated by an individual, city, or nation.

learning_objectives: Define biodiversity at the genetic, species, and ecosystem levels
Explain the primary threats to biodiversity: habitat loss, invasive species, pollution, climate change, overexploitation
Evaluate conservation strategies: protected areas, corridors, and restoration ecology
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 8-9
IUCN Red List Summary Statistics (current year)

lecture_notes.introduction: Biodiversity is the variety of life on Earth and the foundation of ecosystem services humans depend on. We are currently in what scientists call the sixth mass extinction, driven primarily by human activity.
key_concepts: Three levels of biodiversity: genetic, species, ecosystem
Ecosystem services: pollination, water purification, climate regulation, food
HIPPO threats: Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Population growth, Overexploitation
Protected areas and wildlife corridors
The economics of biodiversity: ecosystem services valuation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone and the resulting trophic cascade

review.value: Wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing vegetation recovery along rivers, which stabilized banks, attracted beavers, and increased bird diversity. Top predators can reshape entire ecosystems.
The economic value of global ecosystem services has been estimated at $125-145 trillion per year. Losing biodiversity means losing services that no technology can fully replace.

class_schedule.topic: Climate Change: Science, Causes, and Evidence
learning_objectives: Explain the greenhouse effect and identify major greenhouse gases
Interpret temperature, CO2, and sea level data from scientific sources
Distinguish between natural climate variability and human-caused climate change
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 14
IPCC AR6 Summary for Policymakers (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Climate change is the defining environmental challenge of our time. Understanding the science is the prerequisite for evaluating solutions, policies, and personal choices.
key_concepts: The greenhouse effect: how CO2, methane, and N2O trap heat
Evidence: ice cores, temperature records, sea level data, ocean acidification
Carbon cycle disruption: fossil fuel combustion and deforestation
Climate feedback loops: ice-albedo, water vapor, permafrost methane
Scientific consensus: 97%+ of climate scientists agree on human causation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing 800,000 years of CO2 data from ice cores and correlating with temperature anomalies

review.value: Solar radiation passes through the atmosphere and warms Earth's surface. The surface emits infrared radiation, which greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit, trapping heat. This is natural and necessary; the problem is the enhanced greenhouse effect from additional human emissions.
Warming melts ice, exposing darker ocean or land, which absorbs more heat, causing more warming and more melting. A positive feedback loop that accelerates warming.

class_schedule.topic: Energy Systems: Fossil Fuels and Renewable Alternatives
learning_objectives: Compare the environmental impacts of fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas
Evaluate renewable energy sources: solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal
Analyze the energy transition: challenges, costs, and progress
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 15-16
Our World in Data: Energy Mix by Country (interactive)

lecture_notes.introduction: Energy is the master resource. How we produce and consume energy determines our climate trajectory, air quality, water use, and geopolitics. The transition to clean energy is the largest infrastructure project in human history.
key_concepts: Fossil fuels: formation, extraction, combustion, and environmental costs
Solar energy: photovoltaic and concentrated solar, cost trends
Wind energy: onshore and offshore, intermittency challenges
Energy storage: batteries, pumped hydro, and grid-scale solutions
Life-cycle analysis: comparing total environmental impact of energy sources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive comparison of the energy mix of five countries and discussion of what drives differences
setup_requirements: Our World in Data website access

review.value: Solar PV costs have fallen 90% since 2010, making solar the cheapest source of new electricity in most of the world. The challenge is intermittency and storage.

Compares the total environmental impact of an energy source from raw material extraction through manufacturing, operation, and decommissioning. Nuclear has low carbon emissions but high waste concerns; solar has manufacturing emissions but zero operational emissions.

learning_objectives: Describe the global water cycle and the distribution of freshwater resources
Identify major sources and types of water pollution
Evaluate solutions for water scarcity and pollution control
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 11-12
The Coming Water Crisis (National Geographic feature)

lecture_notes.introduction: Water is essential for all life, yet less than 1% of Earth's water is accessible freshwater. Growing populations, agriculture, industry, and climate change are placing unprecedented strain on water resources.
key_concepts: The water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration
Freshwater distribution: 97.5% saltwater, 2.5% fresh, less than 1% accessible
Point source vs. nonpoint source pollution
Major pollutants: nutrients (eutrophication), pathogens, heavy metals, plastics
Solutions: wastewater treatment, wetland restoration, drip irrigation, desalination
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping global water stress using the World Resources Institute Aqueduct tool

review.value: Excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff causes algae blooms. When algae die and decompose, bacteria consume dissolved oxygen, creating dead zones where aquatic life cannot survive.

The hidden water used to produce goods. A cotton t-shirt requires about 2,700 liters; a kilogram of beef requires about 15,000 liters. Understanding virtual water reveals the true water footprint of consumption.

learning_objectives: Define sustainable development and the three pillars: environment, economy, society
Evaluate the UN Sustainable Development Goals as a framework for action
Identify individual, community, and systemic actions that advance sustainability
readings: Environment: The Science Behind the Stories Ch. 20
UN Sustainable Development Goals Overview (UN website)

lecture_notes.introduction: Sustainability is not about going backward; it is about going forward intelligently. Sustainable development meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
key_concepts: Three pillars of sustainability: environmental, economic, social
UN Sustainable Development Goals: 17 goals for 2030
Circular economy: design out waste, keep materials in use
Systems thinking: understanding interconnections and unintended consequences
Individual action vs. systemic change: both are necessary
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students design a sustainability improvement plan for the university campus using SDG framework

review.value: Replaces the linear 'take-make-dispose' model with closed loops: design for durability, reuse, repair, and recycling. Waste from one process becomes input for another.
Environmental problems are interconnected. A policy to reduce carbon emissions (e.g., biofuel mandates) can create food price increases if not designed with the full system in mind.

questions.question: Explain the 10% rule of energy transfer in ecosystems and use it to explain why there are fewer top predators than herbivores in any ecosystem.
questions.answer: The 10% rule states that only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next level. The rest is lost as heat through metabolic processes. If producers capture 10,000 kcal of energy, primary consumers (herbivores) receive approximately 1,000 kcal, secondary consumers receive 100 kcal, and tertiary consumers (top predators) receive only 10 kcal. Because each level has dramatically less available energy, it can support fewer and fewer organisms. Top predators are rare because the ecosystem simply cannot produce enough energy to sustain large populations at the top of the food chain.

rubric.key: Quantitative example showing energy loss across levels
Connects energy limitation to predator population size

questions.question: Describe two positive feedback loops that accelerate climate change and explain why they make climate change harder to reverse.
questions.answer: Feedback loop 1: Ice-albedo feedback. Warming melts Arctic ice, exposing darker ocean water that absorbs more solar radiation instead of reflecting it. This causes more warming, which melts more ice, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Feedback loop 2: Permafrost methane feedback. Warming thaws Arctic permafrost, releasing trapped methane (a greenhouse gas 80 times more potent than CO2 over 20 years). The released methane causes more warming, which thaws more permafrost and releases more methane. These loops make climate change harder to reverse because they continue operating even if human emissions stop. The warming already caused triggers natural processes that generate additional warming independently of human activity.

rubric.key: Explains why feedback loops make reversal difficult

questions.question: Compare solar and wind energy as replacements for fossil fuels. For each, identify one major advantage and one major challenge. Then explain why energy storage is critical for both.
questions.answer: Solar advantage: The cost of solar PV has dropped 90% since 2010 and is now the cheapest new electricity source in most regions. Solar challenge: Intermittency; solar panels produce no electricity at night and reduced output on cloudy days. Wind advantage: Wind turbines produce zero emissions during operation and can be built offshore where winds are strongest and land use conflicts are minimal. Wind challenge: Intermittency; wind speed is variable and unpredictable, meaning output fluctuates. Energy storage is critical for both because electricity grids must match supply to demand in real time. Without storage, solar and wind can only provide power when conditions are favorable. Battery storage, pumped hydro, and other technologies bridge the gap between when renewable energy is produced and when it is needed.

rubric.key: One valid advantage and challenge for solar
One valid advantage and challenge for wind
Explains why storage is critical for grid reliability

questions.question: What is eutrophication? Describe the process from nutrient source to dead zone and explain why it is classified as a nonpoint source pollution problem.
questions.answer: Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of water bodies with nutrients, primarily nitrogen and phosphorus. The process: (1) Fertilizers from agricultural fields wash into rivers during rain events. (2) Nutrients flow downstream into lakes or coastal waters. (3) Excess nutrients trigger explosive algae growth (algal blooms). (4) When algae die, bacteria decompose the organic matter, consuming dissolved oxygen. (5) Oxygen levels drop below what aquatic organisms need, creating a 'dead zone' where fish and other marine life cannot survive. It is classified as nonpoint source pollution because the nutrients do not come from a single identifiable discharge point like a pipe. Instead, they originate from diffuse sources across large agricultural areas, making the pollution difficult to trace to any single farm or field.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of nonpoint source classification

major_assignments.title: Campus Sustainability Audit and Action Plan
major_assignments.description: Conduct a sustainability audit of one aspect of the university campus (energy use, water use, waste management, food services, or transportation) and develop a data-driven action plan with specific, measurable recommendations for improvement.
objectives: Apply environmental science concepts to evaluate a real-world system
Collect and analyze data related to resource use and environmental impact
Communicate findings effectively to a non-technical audience
deliverables: Audit report (1500-2000 words) with data tables and analysis
At least 5 data points collected through observation, research, or campus records
Three specific, measurable sustainability recommendations with cost-benefit analysis
Visual presentation (infographic or poster) summarizing findings for campus display
Implementation timeline with milestones for each recommendation

criteria: At least 5 relevant data points collected
Data sources are credible and documented
Methodology for collection is described
Data is presented clearly in tables or figures
Environmental science concepts applied correctly
Current performance compared to benchmarks or standards
Root causes of problems identified
Analysis supports the recommendations
Three specific, actionable recommendations
Cost-benefit analysis for each recommendation
Implementation timeline is realistic
Environmental impact quantified where possible
Visual presentation is clear and compelling
Key findings communicated to non-technical audience
Professional design and formatting
Accurate representation of data
Thorough data-driven audit of a campus sustainability challenge
Strong connection between environmental science concepts and practical findings
Actionable recommendations with realistic cost-benefit analysis
Clear, professional communication of findings to a broad audience

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of environmental science concepts and data interpretation

lab_activities.description: Hands-on data collection, analysis, and ecological field work

sustainability_audit.description: Campus sustainability audit with data-driven action plan

weekly_news_analysis.description: Weekly analysis of environmental news using course concepts

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam covering all environmental science topics

course_policies.attendance: Field activities and lab sessions require attendance. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower your final grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Lab reports and news analyses lose 10% per day late. The sustainability audit has staged deadlines that must be met for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab work is collaborative; written reports are individual. Data may be collected in groups but analysis and writing must be your own.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabricating data or copying analyses is academic dishonesty and will result in a zero on the assignment. All sources must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for data tools and research. Spreadsheet software for data analysis. Smartphone or camera for field documentation.
